sections.title: Rise of a Trading Empire
sections.body:
In the highlands of northern Ethiopia, at the crossroads of Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean world, a kingdom arose that would rival the greatest powers of the ancient world. The Kingdom of Aksum — known to the Romans, Persians, and Chinese alike — was no isolated realm. It was a cosmopolitan empire built on trade, faith, and stone.

The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, a Greek merchant's guide written around 100 CE, describes the port of Adulis as a thriving international market where ivory, gold, tortoiseshell, obsidian, frankincense, and myrrh were traded for textiles, glass, wine, and metals from across the known world.

Aksum's strategic position — controlling the Red Sea coast and the highland trade routes into the African interior — made it indispensable. Merchants from Egypt, Arabia, India, and even Ceylon passed through its ports. The kingdom grew wealthy beyond measure.
From Adulis to the city of the Axumites, the journey takes eight days. The city is the seat of the king and the greatest market of all the towns in that region.
sections.attribution: — Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, circa 100 CE

sections.title: Kings and Coinage
sections.body:
Aksum was the first African kingdom south of the Sahara to mint its own coins — a mark of sovereignty and economic sophistication that placed it alongside Rome, Persia, and Kushan India in the ranks of the world's great monetary powers.

Beginning with King Endubis around 270 CE, Aksumite coins were struck in gold, silver, and bronze. They bore the king's image on one side — often wearing a crown and holding a scepter — and the Aksumite cross or crescent on the reverse. The inscriptions were in Greek, the lingua franca of the eastern Mediterranean and Red Sea trade networks.

The coins were not merely currency; they were instruments of state propaganda. Each new king issued coins bearing his own image and titles, announcing to the world: 'I rule Aksum.' The gold coins in particular circulated as far as India, a testament to the empire's reach.

sections.caption: Aksumite gold coin showing King Endubis, circa 270–300 CE. The Greek inscription reads 'King Endubis of the Aksumites.'

sections.title: The City of Aksum
sections.body:
The capital city of Aksum was a marvel of its age. At its heart stood the great obelisks — monolithic granite stelae carved from single blocks of stone, the largest standing 33 meters tall and weighing over 500 tons. These were not merely monuments; they were engineering achievements that exceeded anything being built in sub-Saharan Africa at the time.

The city sprawled across the highland plateau, with multi-story stone palaces, elaborate underground tombs, reservoirs, and a sophisticated water management system. Foreign visitors described it as a city of 'great houses and palaces of stone' — a description at odds with later European assumptions about pre-colonial Africa.

Beneath the stelae field, archaeologists discovered a vast necropolis — the Tomb of the False Door, the Tomb of the Brick Arches — containing precious artifacts from across the ancient world: Roman wine goblets, Indian beads, Chinese ceramics, and Arabian incense burners. Aksum was not isolated; it was connected.
The Aksumites are a civilized people. They have great cities, stone houses, and kings who rule with justice. Their port of Adulis is filled with ships from every nation.

sections.title: Trade, Culture, and Global Connection
sections.body:
Aksum's wealth was built on its mastery of two worlds: the African interior and the Indian Ocean maritime trade. From the interior came ivory — the empire's most lucrative export — along with gold, slaves, rhino horn, and hippopotamus hides. From the coast, these goods were shipped to Egypt, Arabia, India, and beyond.

The Aksumites developed their own written script, Ge'ez, which remains the liturgical language of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church today. They built temples to indigenous gods like Astar, Beher, and Medr — deities of heaven, earth, and sea — before Christianity transformed the kingdom.

Their architecture was uniquely Aksumite: the 'monkey-head' building style with alternating projecting and recessed wooden beams set into stone walls, an aesthetic that would define Ethiopian architecture for the next two millennia. Walk through any Ethiopian church today and you see echoes of Aksum's builders.

sections.title: What Comes Next
sections.body: In the next chapter: the reign of King Ezana — the emperor who converted to Christianity, making Aksum one of the first Christian kingdoms in the world (alongside Armenia). Ezana's conquest of Meroë, his inscriptions in three languages, and his transformation of Aksum from a trading empire into a Christian kingdom would alter the course of African and world history.
